# ADR-260031: Dual-View Accessibility and Usability Baseline

## Workflow

- This ADR records the accepted accessibility and usability baseline for dual graph views.
- It defines keyboard navigation behavior, tab semantics/ARIA, contrast and non-color state signaling, and legend/tooltip discoverability requirements.
- This ADR refines ADR-260028 and ADR-260030 by adding interaction accessibility constraints.

Status: Accepted
Date: 2026-05-12
Owners: Repository owner, product collaborator

## Context

Dual representations increase interaction complexity. Without explicit accessibility/usability rules, users can lose orientation, keyboard users may be blocked, and visual semantics may be ambiguous.

An MVP baseline is needed to guarantee practical accessibility and maintain user confidence while preserving implementation scope.

## Decision

### D1: Keyboard Navigation Baseline
- Tab sequence must include top bar, search controls, view tabs, graph canvas focus target, and side panels.
- Arrow-key navigation is supported for graph-node focus traversal in active view.
- Enter/Space activates selection on focused node.
- Escape clears node focus context without mutating data.

### D2: Tab Semantics and Focus Visibility
- View switcher uses WAI-ARIA tab pattern (`tablist`, `tab`, `tabpanel`).
- Active tab uses correct selected semantics and control linkage.
- Visible focus indicator is required for tabs and graph focus targets.

### D3: State Signaling and Contrast Rules
- Key text and controls target WCAG 2.1 AA contrast.
- Node/edge states must not rely on color alone.
- Selected/hover/connected/error states must include secondary non-color cue (outline, icon, shape, stroke style, or label).

### D4: Discoverability Aids
- Node tooltip on hover/focus includes full label and type.
- Edge tooltip on hover/focus includes relationship label/type and direction (when directional).
- A lightweight graph legend explains node/edge states and connector-handle meaning.

## Alternatives Considered

- Option A: Defer keyboard graph navigation to post-MVP. Rejected: creates immediate accessibility gap.
- Option B: Use visual color states only. Rejected: insufficient accessibility and ambiguous for many users.
- Option C: Remove legend to reduce UI density. Rejected for MVP: hurts discoverability of new visual grammar.

## Consequences

### Positive
- Improves accessibility for keyboard and assistive-technology users.
- Reduces ambiguity in dual-view state interpretation.
- Improves onboarding/discoverability for Network view interactions.

### Negative
- Adds UI behavior/testing surface.
- Requires tighter discipline in focus management and ARIA wiring.

### Risks
- Keyboard node navigation may require iterative tuning for dense graphs.
- Overly verbose tooltips/legend copy could increase cognitive load if not concise.

## Verification

- Verify full keyboard path through shell, tabs, graph, and side panels.
- Verify ARIA tab semantics and selected-state correctness.
- Verify visible focus indicators under keyboard-only usage.
- Verify non-color cues are present for core node/edge states.
- Verify tooltips and legend expose expected state semantics.

## Rollback

- If graph keyboard traversal becomes unstable, keep tab + shell accessibility and temporarily constrain node-level traversal while maintaining basic keyboard operability.
- If legend footprint is excessive, collapse to a toggle while preserving access to the same semantics.
